turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has our opinion in case 17-459, Pereira v. Sessions.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The Attorney General of the United States has discretion to grant a noncitizen a form of discretionary relief known as Cancellation of Removal. To be eligible for such relief a noncitizen resident must satisfy certain statutory criteria. The relevant one here being that the noncitizen must have been physically present in the United States for a continuous period of at least 10 years, under what is often called the Stop-Time Rule; however, a noncitizen's period of physical presence is deemed to end when the noncitizen is served a Notice to Appear under Section 1229(a). Section 1229(a) provides that the government shall serve noncitizens in removal proceedings with a Notice to Appear specifying several pieces of information including the time and place at which the removal proceedings will be held. This case arises against that complex statutory background. In this case the government sought to remove petitioner Wescley Pereira, a citizen and native of Brazil from the United States and therefore served him with a document entitled Notice to Appear. The notice did not specify the date and time of Pereira's removal hearing. Instead it ordered him to appear before an immigration judge in Boston on a date and time to be set in the future. The question in this case is whether a notice that fails to specify the time or place of removal proceedings just like the one the government served on Pereira triggers him to Stop-Time Rule. The answer is no. A notice is, that does not inform a noncitizen when and where to appear for removal proceedings is not a notice to appear under Section 1229(a) and therefore does not trigger the Stop-Time Rule. The statutory text alone resolves this case. Under the Stop-Time Rule a noncitizen's period of physical presence when he has served "a notice to appear under Section 1229(a)" by expressly referencing Section 1229(a) the statute tells us where to look to find the meaning of the term Notice to Appear. Section 1229(a)(1) then clarifies that a Notice to Appear is a written notice specified as relevant here "the time and place at which the removal proceedings will be held." Accordingly based on the plain statutory text it is clear to trigger the Stop-Time Rule, a Notice to Appear must at least specify the time and place of the removal proceeding. The surrounding statutory context reinforces the straightforward interpretation of the Stop-Time Rule. For instance, Section 1229(a)(2) provides that in the case of any change or postponement in the time and place of removal proceedings the government shall give the noncitizen written notice specifying the new time or place of the proceedings. This provision necessarily presumes that the government has already served a Notice to Appear specifying the time and place of the removal proceedings, otherwise there would be no time or place to change or postpone. Thus, Section 1229(a)(2) strongly supports the conclusion that a Notice to Appear under Section 1229(a) must include at least the time and place of the removal proceedings to trigger the Stop-Time Rule. Basic common sense also bolsters our reading of the statute. If the words Notice to Appear mean anything in this context, they must mean that the government must at least provide noncitizens with the minimum amount of information, the time and place that would enable them to appear at the removal proceedings in the first place. In a misguided effort to inject ambiguity into the statute the government and the dissent advance a litany of overlapping counter arguments, but as we explained in detail in our opinion none of these argument provides a convincing basis to depart from the plain language of the statute. For these and other reasons set forth in our opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Court of Appeals for the First Circuit and remand for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. Justice Kennedy has filed a concurring opinion, Justice Alito has filed a dissenting opinion.
